coaching.subject: tickets-branch reachability verification command

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for formal dependency edges on upstream signing stories — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: formal dependency edges on upstream signing stories

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A"` with a command that also asserts branch reachability, e.g. `git -C .tickets-tracker merge-base --is-ancestor "$A" HEAD` (exit 0 when run with the tickets branch checked out) or `git -C .tickets-tracker branch --contains "$A" | grep -q tickets`.
evidence: 'git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A" prints commit (the anchor exists on the tickets branch)'
cat-file -t checks object type, not branch reachability — verify-command defect #4 (fixture invalidity / wrong command for the claim)
findings.finding: The AC3 proving command `git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A"` only verifies the SHA refers to a commit object in the object store; it does NOT verify the commit is reachable from (i.e. on) the tickets branch. A SHA that exists in the local object store but is not an ancestor of the tickets-branch tip would still print `commit` and falsely pass the check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, proving command: `git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A"` prints `commit`
scenarios: A detached-HEAD commit or a commit fetched from another ref could satisfy the check while not being a proper tickets-branch commit, causing the anchor to be unusable by 8d39.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the proving command with:
```
git -C .tickets-tracker merge-base --is-ancestor "$A" "$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse tickets)" && echo 'anchor is on tickets branch'
```
or, if tickets is the default branch:
```
git -C .tickets-tracker branch --contains "$A" | grep -q .
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency declaration (e.g. `depends_on: [79da, 4dc0, 1a51]`) or a gating note in the plan stating that AC1 cannot be completed until those three stories are merged, so the executor knows to sequence this story after them.
evidence: 'after those three stories confirm signing, choose the anchor'
'local 79da, CI 4dc0, Gerrit-side 1a51' — named but not declared as blocking dependencies
AC1 proving command requires a live signed history to exit 0
findings.finding: The anchor-decision step (AC1) is explicitly sequenced *after* stories 79da, 4dc0, and 1a51 confirm signing, but no formal dependency edge is declared in the plan. If those stories are not yet merged when this story is picked up, AC1 cannot be completed in-session — the executor has no stated default action (e.g. 'block until dependencies land').
findings.location: What section — 'Decide and document the enforcement-boundary anchor'; AC1 proving command
scenarios: Executor picks up this story while 79da/4dc0/1a51 are still in-flight; cannot choose a valid anchor; AC1 remains uncompletable for the session.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or Why section: 'Prerequisite: stories 79da, 4dc0, and 1a51 must be merged before the anchor can be chosen (AC1). This story is blocked on those three.' Also add `depends_on: [79da, 4dc0, 1a51]` as plan metadata if the tracker supports it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the story to remove 'repin' (which implies a config write) in favour of wording that reflects the actual deliverables — e.g. 'Verify identity.enforce_since grandfathering logic, add regression test, and decide enforcement-boundary anchor' — so the title matches the explicitly write-free scope.
evidence: Title: 'Verify and repin identity.enforce_since so pre-flip events are grandfathered'
Body Scope: 'Writing enforce_since into rebar.toml and the flag flip both belong to 8d39 — this story does not modify rebar.toml'
AC: 'The anchor is chosen and recorded; rebar.toml is untouched'
The word 'repin' connotes writing/setting a value, not merely deciding it
findings.finding: The title says 'repin identity.enforce_since' (implying an active write of the anchor value) but the plan body explicitly scopes out that write to story 8d39, making this story deliver only a decision/documentation artifact. An implementer reading only the title could expect `rebar.toml` or a config file to be updated as part of this story's output.
findings.location: Plan title / Scope section / AC line 3 ('The anchor is chosen and recorded; rebar.toml is untouched')
scenarios: An implementer reading the title in isolation attempts to write enforce_since in this story, creating a conflict with 8d39 which is the designated single-writer.
A reviewer approving 8d39 is confused about whether 'repin' already occurred in this story.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Verify identity.enforce_since grandfathering logic and decide enforcement-boundary anchor for pre-flip events'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite declaration (e.g. 'Depends on: 79da, 4dc0, 1a51') and clarify in AC1/AC3 that anchor verification is gated on those stories completing signing confirmation, or split the anchor-decision step into a separate follow-on task.
evidence: What section: 'after those three stories confirm signing, choose the anchor as a tickets-branch commit that every pre-signing unsigned event strictly precedes'
AC1: 'rebar verify-identity --all --require-authenticated --since <anchor> exits 0' — implies anchor is already chosen
AC3: 'git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A" prints commit' and 'a comment on this ticket records A + its rationale' — implies anchor is already decided and recorded
No 'depends on 79da, 4dc0, 1a51' prerequisite stated anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The 'What' section makes the anchor-decision step contingent on three other stories (79da, 4dc0, 1a51) confirming signing before the anchor can be chosen, but the Acceptance Criteria (AC1 and AC3) present the anchor as a verifiable deliverable of this story with no stated prerequisite on those stories. An implementer cannot satisfy AC1 ('rebar verify-identity --since <anchor> exits 0') or AC3 ('git -C .tickets-tracker cat-file -t "$A" prints commit' + ticket comment) until those three stories complete, yet no prerequisite or sequencing guard appears in the plan.
findings.location: What section ('Decide and document the enforcement-boundary anchor') vs AC1 and AC3
scenarios: An implementer picks up this story while 79da/4dc0/1a51 are in progress; they cannot choose the anchor, so AC1 and AC3 are un-closeable, but the plan gives no guidance on whether to block, partially close, or split the story.
A reviewer tries to verify AC1/AC3 before the three writer-signing stories are done and finds the anchor not yet chosen — unclear if the story is incomplete or the AC is premature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites' section: 'This story's anchor-decision step (AC1, AC3) cannot be completed until stories 79da, 4dc0, and 1a51 confirm signing is live across all writers. AC2 (regression test) is independently completable.' Alternatively, scope the anchor-decision out of this story entirely and make it a dependency of 8d39.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the inclusive-boundary semantic (anchor commit is enforced, not grandfathered, because `git merge-base --is-ancestor <ref> <ref>` exits 0) in a persistent location — a comment block on `_is_enforced`, a CLAUDE.md entry, or a brief ADR — so future maintainers don't have to rediscover it from a closed ticket.
evidence: '`git merge-base --is-ancestor <anchor> <commit>`, which exits 0 when `commit == anchor`, so the anchor commit is enforced'
Scope: 'the change touches only the new test file under tests/' — no ADR or CLAUDE.md update planned
ABSENCE: no mention of updating any persistent design document, ADR, or CLAUDE.md with this boundary-inclusion rule
findings.finding: The inclusive-boundary semantic — that the anchor commit itself is ENFORCED (not grandfathered), because `git merge-base --is-ancestor <anchor> <anchor>` exits 0 — is a non-obvious business rule documented only in the ephemeral ticket text. No persistent design doc, ADR, or CLAUDE.md update is planned to capture this decision for future maintainers who may need to reason about or change the boundary logic.
findings.location: AC: 'The chosen anchor grandfathers all pre-signing history' / What section: `_is_enforced` description / Scope
scenarios: A future developer adjusting the enforcement boundary assumes off-by-one behavior (exclusive anchor) and introduces a regression, because the rationale is only in a closed ticket.
A reviewer of `_is_enforced` misreads the `merge-base --is-ancestor` semantics and 'fixes' it to `--is-ancestor <commit> <anchor>` (reversed), silently changing boundary behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring or block comment directly on the `_is_enforced` function (verify_authorship.py:262-282) stating: 'NOTE: the boundary is inclusive — a commit equal to since_ref is considered enforced (git merge-base --is-ancestor X X exits 0). The anchor must therefore be a commit strictly after all unsigned history.' This requires no file beyond the existing scope if the test or source already touches that function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' section explaining why `git merge-base --is-ancestor` (git ancestry) was chosen as the grandfathering predicate — e.g. that commit timestamps are not monotone and can be spoofed/rewritten, making ancestry the only tamper-resistant boundary on a git-backed store.
evidence: The plan describes the mechanism ('enforces an event iff its introducing commit is since_ref or a descendant') but gives no stated reason WHY git ancestry was chosen over alternatives such as timestamp ordering or a wall-clock cutoff.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'git ancestry was chosen because...' or 'this approach is preferred over timestamp-based checks because...' appears anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing git ancestry (`merge-base --is-ancestor`) as the grandfathering mechanism — i.e. why this approach fits over simpler or alternative boundary checks. G6 requires the plan to affirmatively explain why the chosen approach was selected, not just describe what it does.
scenarios: An implementer who questions the mechanism has no rationale to fall back on; a reviewer in a future pass cannot distinguish 'chosen deliberately' from 'chosen by default'.
If a future maintainer considers a timestamp-based boundary (e.g. event creation time), the plan gives no documented reason to prefer the ancestry approach.
findings.suggested_fix: In the bullet describing `_is_enforced`, add: 'Git ancestry is used (rather than timestamp or wall-clock comparisons) because commit author/committer timestamps are not monotone and are trivially spoofable; ancestry on the tickets branch is the only tamper-resistant ordering guarantee available in this store model.'

findings.finding: AC1 states 'every event at/before the anchor is grandfathered', but the code's `_is_enforced` logic enforces events AT the boundary commit (because `git merge-base --is-ancestor X X` exits 0 — a commit is its own ancestor, so `since_ref == commit_sha` → ENFORCED). The correct semantic is: events STRICTLY BEFORE the anchor are grandfathered; the anchor commit itself and all descendants are enforced. The AC language 'at/before' inverts the boundary-commit treatment.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1 ('The chosen anchor grandfathers all pre-boundary history')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'every event at/before the anchor is grandfathered' with 'every event whose introducing commit is a STRICT ancestor of the anchor is grandfathered (the anchor commit itself is enforced)'. In the boundary-selection procedure, add: after `rebar verify-identity --all --require-authenticated --since <candidate>` exits 0, that already confirms no events at/after the candidate are unsigned-and-enforced, so the chosen candidate is safe — but document explicitly that unsigned events introduced IN the candidate commit would be enforced.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
